source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My team is building a music streaming app where users browse songs. We want them to be able to quickly view a song's details and maybe create playlists. What's a good structure for this?

The Media Browser pattern is suitable for your music streaming app. It provides a grid of objects (songs, in this case) and a separate view for a single item. Users can easily go back and forth between browsing the grid and viewing song details. You can also offer playlist functionality directly at the Media Browser level, allowing users to create playlists as they browse. Be mindful of clutter; display only essential metadata like title or artist with each song in the grid.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: For a document management system, how can I help users quickly find and open specific files from a large collection, and also allow them to reorder them if they own them?

Implement a Media Browser pattern with a browsing interface. This pattern allows users to explore a large collection easily. For finding specific files, consider adding a Search box to the browsing interface. When a user clicks on a file, display it immediately in a single-item view. If the user owns the files, provide ways to move or reorder them at the Media Browser level, which implies supporting multiple selections.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a photo management app for desktop. Users can organize photos into albums. How should I let them browse their photos?

For a desktop photo management app, let users browse their filesystem for images stored in different directories. If users can group items into albums, make these collections available in a browsing interface. Most users also appreciate the ability to favorite or star items. Consider putting filters into the browsing interface, allowing users to slice through large collections using properties like keywords or modification dates.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We're building a video streaming website similar to YouTube. How should we display videos on the homepage and when a user clicks on a specific video?

For a public video collection website like YouTube, use the entire home page as a browsing interface. Present a balanced view with user-owned and promoted content. When a user clicks on a video, display a single-item view that uses the entire screen for playback. You can also show a large view of the video next to a media browser grid if the window is large enough. Display metadata about the video alongside it. On the single-item view, offer social features like comments, liking, and sharing, as well as tagging or labeling. Consider an 'other items you may like' feature. Editing features for individual videos can also reside here. Provide 'previous' and 'next' buttons to navigate between videos, especially if using a one-window drilldown for the single-item view.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: For our new mobile app that displays a large collection of articles, how can we make it easy for users to browse and select articles?

For a large collection of articles on a mobile app, consider using a grid of images, perhaps with brief written descriptions, as a compact and useful pattern for browsing and selecting. This pattern is universal across mobile interfaces. Selecting an item from the grid can either load the article for direct consumption or load a detail screen with a description. To aid comprehension with a large number of items, group the articles into categories.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a photo editing app for desktop. Users need to browse and manage their image collections. What layout pattern should I consider?

Consider using the Two-Panel Selector pattern, as seen in applications like Apple Photos and Adobe Bridge. This pattern is effective for managing collections of images. You can vary the complexity, from a simpler design like Apple Photos to a more complex one with numerous panels and filters like Adobe Bridge, depending on your needs.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My team is building a video editing tool for desktop. We need a way for users to manage their video clips and assemble them. What layout pattern would work?

For managing time-based media like videos, consider using a ribbon layout, which is a single-height row that scrolls. In applications like Apple iMovie, this functions as a timeline and serves as the central working palette for creating time-based media. This approach is suitable for assembling and processing video assets.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We're designing a media management tool for desktop. Users will be working with images and videos. How can we structure the interface to allow users to view individual items and the overall collection?

You can adapt patterns like the Two-Panel Selector, which is used by applications like Apple Photos and Adobe Bridge for managing image collections. For reaching a single-item view, Apple Photos uses One-Window Drilldown. Alternatively, applications like Adobe Bridge and Apple iMovie present multiple views together on one page, which might be suitable if you want to combine collection browsing and single-item editing on a single screen.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a new financial tracking app for mobile. Users need to see their key financial metrics at a glance when they open the app. What kind of screen should I use?

Consider using a dashboard. A dashboard is often the first screen users see and is designed to display key data points on a single, information-dense page. This is beneficial because it provides users with relevant, actionable information, such as charts, graphs, and important messages or links related to key metrics or workflows. Dashboards are familiar and recognizable, setting user expectations for quick status updates and task overviews.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My team is building a business intelligence tool. We want users to be able to quickly see the status of various business operations. What's a good pattern for this?

A dashboard pattern would be highly effective for your business intelligence tool. Dashboards are excellent for displaying key data points on a single page, updated regularly, to provide users with relevant and actionable information. They are popular because they solve the need for continuous monitoring of incoming information and offer a quick update on status and tasks to be done. Dashboards often incorporate other patterns like titled sections, tabs, collapsible panels, and information graphics.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We're developing a content management system where users frequently need to reorder, delete, or insert pages within a document. What interface pattern could help with this?

For managing document pages, a grid view, similar to what Adobe Acrobat offers in its edit mode, would be beneficial. This pattern allows for rapid reorganization of pages, selection for deletion, or choosing an insertion point for adding new screens. It provides a visual overview that facilitates these page management tasks.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a dashboard for a project management tool. Users need to see their overdue tasks. What's the best way to present this?

Feature the most important information, like overdue tasks, prominently. Use a good visual hierarchy to arrange lists or tables. Consider highlighting keywords and numbers so they stand out from surrounding text, as users will try to get actionable information at a glance. Avoid overwhelming them with too much data; eliminate confusing or unimportant data so they can pick out what matters.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We have a lot of performance metrics for our app. How should I organize them on a dashboard so users can understand them quickly?

Group related data into Titled Sections to make it easier to digest. Try to keep the main information on one page with little or no scrolling, so users can see everything at a glance. If you're using information graphics, choose appropriate ones. Simple line and bar charts are often better for expressing comparative or time-based data than gauges or pie charts.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: Users of our financial app need to see detailed transaction history, but also a summary. How can I balance this?

Use One-Window Drilldown to let users see additional details about the data. They should be able to click links or graphics to find out more about specific transactions, while keeping the main summary visible. Datatips can also work well to show individual data points when a pointer rolls over an information graphic.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a new photo editing app for desktop. What's a good way to organize the tools so users can easily create and manipulate images?

Consider using the Canvas Plus Palette pattern. This involves a large, central workspace (the canvas) where users create or edit their digital objects, surrounded by grids of tools (palettes) represented as icons. This is effective for graphical editors because it mirrors familiar physical objects like an artist's easel, leveraging visual recognition for common tools like paintbrushes or magnifying glasses. Present the canvas prominently and place palettes with iconic buttons to the left or top. You can group tools using Module Tabs or Collapsible Panels if needed. Most buttons should create objects on the canvas, but you can also integrate tools for actions like zooming or selecting.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My team is building a vector graphics editor for web. We want to make sure users can quickly access editing functions. How should we structure the interface?

The Canvas Plus Palette pattern is well-suited for your vector graphics editor. It provides a large, empty canvas for users to work on and arranges tools in palettes around it. This structure is familiar to most desktop software users and leverages visual recognition, as icons for tools like selection or drawing are often reused across applications. Ensure the canvas is the central focus, and place palettes with iconic buttons to the left or top. You can add text labels to icons if they are not immediately clear, and consider using Module Tabs or Collapsible Panels to organize subgroups of tools.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a drawing app for tablets. We have a lot of tools, and I'm wondering how to best present them to the user. Should I use a single palette or multiple?

For applications with a large number of tools or choices, consider using multiple palettes. This pattern is seen in applications like iOS Photos, where multiple editing tools are organized within open-close panels below the main canvas. This approach helps manage complexity and can make the tools more accessible.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: For our new photo editing app, we're debating how users should select and apply tools from the palette to the canvas. What are some common interaction patterns?

Interaction patterns for creating items on a palette can vary. Some applications rely solely on drag-and-drop. Others use a single click on the palette followed by a single click on the canvas. You might also consider pressure-sensitive tools, especially if you're designing for touch interfaces where varying finger pressure can be used. Usability testing is crucial here because these behaviors might not be immediately obvious or could be difficult to learn.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We're building a wireframing tool for desktop. Where should we place the palettes for tools and components?

Palettes can be strategically placed around the central drawing pane. For instance, Axure RP Pro uses palettes on both the left and right sides of the main pane where users create their wireframes. This arrangement keeps the tools accessible without obstructing the primary workspace.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a checkout flow for an e-commerce app. Should I put all the steps like shipping, billing, and payment on one page or break them into separate screens?

You can break up the operations constituting the checkout task into a series of chunks, or groups of operations. For an online purchase, a thematic breakdown can include screens for product selection, payment information, a billing address, and a shipping address. The presentation order doesn't much matter because later choices don't depend on earlier choices. Putting related choices together simplifies things for people filling out those forms. Alternatively, you could keep all the steps on one page using Titled Sections with prominent numbers, Responsive Enabling (where steps are disabled until the previous one is finished), or Progressive Disclosure (where steps appear only after the previous one is completed). Progressive Disclosure can be elegant for short checkouts, being dynamic, compact, and easy to use.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We're building a software installer and want to allow users to choose optional packages. How should we structure the UI to handle these choices and the subsequent steps?

You might decide to split up the task at decision points so that choices made by the user can change the downstream steps dynamically. In a software installation Wizard, for example, the user might choose to install optional packages that require yet more choices; if they choose not to do a custom installation, those steps are skipped. Dynamic UIs are good at presenting branched tasks such as this because the user never needs to see anything that’s irrelevant to the choices they made.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a multi-step form for a loan application. Users are getting overwhelmed. What are some ways to manage the complexity of the steps?

The difficult part of designing this kind of UI is striking a balance between the sizes of the chunks and the number of them. It’s silly to have a two-step Wizard, whereas one comprising 15 steps is tedious. On the other hand, each chunk shouldn’t be overwhelmingly large, or you’ve lost some benefits of this pattern. Consider breaking up the operations into a series of chunks, or groups of operations. If you use separate pages for each step, allow the user to move back and forth at will through the task sequence, offering a way to step backward or change their mind about an earlier choice. Many UIs also show a selectable map or overview of all the steps.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a new desktop application with many configuration options. Where should users go to change these settings, and how should I organize them?

You should implement a Settings Editor. This is a self-contained page or window where users can change settings. If you have a large number of settings, divide them into separate tabs or pages. Group properties into well-labeled categories that make immediate sense to aid findability. Users need random access to change settings, not a prescribed sequence like a wizard, and they also use it to view existing settings, so ensure values are communicated at a glance.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We're building a mobile app where users manage their accounts and profiles. What's the best way to let them edit this information?

A Settings Editor is appropriate for this. Users expect to find account and profile settings in familiar places and have them behave in familiar ways. Ensure the editor is findable, and organize the editable information into logical, well-labeled categories. Users will need to view their current settings as well as change them, so make sure the current values are easily visible.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My team is creating an online product configurator. Users need to customize many aspects of the product. How should we structure the interface for this?

A Settings Editor pattern is suitable for your product configurator. This pattern allows users to change properties and preferences. Since there can be many options, consider dividing the settings into separate tabs or pages. It's crucial that users can access and modify any setting directly (random access) rather than being forced through a step-by-step process. Also, ensure that users can easily see the current configuration at a glance.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm building a mobile app for users to set up a new smart home device. It's a complex process with many steps. Should I use a wizard?

A wizard is a good pattern for tasks that are long or complicated and likely novel for users, like setting up a new device. It leads users step-by-step, which can simplify the process by breaking it into discrete chunks. This works well when users prefer to be guided and don't need fine-grained control, similar to following signs in an unfamiliar airport. However, be aware that wizards can feel rigid and limiting to expert users, and they don't always show users how their choices affect the application state. If the setup can be simplified to a short form or a few button clicks, that might be a better, less patronizing solution.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We're designing a desktop application for professional graphic designers. They need to import and process a series of complex image files. Is a wizard the right approach here?

For a task like importing and processing complex image files for professional graphic designers, a wizard might not be the best approach. While wizards are useful for guiding users through long or complicated tasks they do infrequently, they can be frustratingly rigid and limiting for expert users, especially in creative processes. Designers often want more control and to understand how their actions affect the application state, which a wizard typically doesn't provide. Consider if the task can be simplified or if a more flexible interface would better suit their needs.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My users are new to our software and need to complete a multi-stage data migration. They don't do this often and just want it done correctly. Should I use a wizard?

Yes, a wizard pattern is well-suited for this scenario. Since the data migration is multi-stage and likely novel for your users, a wizard can 'divide and conquer' the task into manageable steps. This provides a preplanned roadmap, sparing users the effort of figuring out the task's structure. They can simply address each step in turn, trusting the process. This approach aligns with users who prefer not to think too much and just want instructions on what to do next, especially when they don't have much experience or desire for fine-grained control over the migration process.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a settings screen for a mobile app. Should I use a long scrolling list or categories?

For a mobile OS like iOS, a single scrolling list can work. Apple places critical and frequently used settings at the top to help users. Grouping items within the list also aids navigation and selection. Consider if your app's settings can be ordered this way and if grouping will make it easier for users to find what they need.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My desktop application has many system settings. What's a good way to organize them?

For a desktop OS like macOS, organizing system settings into categories using panels is an effective approach. Use sections, icons, and labels to clearly define these categories. This helps users understand the options available and select the correct one more easily.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing the settings page for a mobile app where users manage their notification preferences. There are quite a few options. How should I organize them?

Group notification preferences into pages with names that make it easy to guess what's on them. For a large number of properties, consider a hierarchy of groups, but be careful not to frustrate users with too many clicks to reach common settings. Tabs are a common layout for presenting these pages, as seen in examples like Google and Facebook for their profile editors. Ensure strong information architecture and navigation to bring understandable structure to these settings.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: For our e-commerce website, users need to access their order history, payment methods, and personal details. What's a good way to present these account settings?

Consider a Menu Page layout, similar to Amazon's 'Your Account,' which lists various account settings alongside other related information like order history and payment management. This approach can bring understandable structure to a complicated part of the platform. If there are many properties, Amazon also uses a list of shortcuts on the top-level page for frequently accessed items and a search box to help users find what they seek.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: On our web application, users can change various settings. Should we apply changes immediately or use a Save button?

The decision to immediately apply changes or offer Save and Cancel buttons can depend on the type of settings. Website settings mostly use Save buttons, while platform-wide settings are often applied immediately. Application settings and preferences can go either way. If there's an established convention or a requirement from the underlying technology, follow that. If you still have open questions, test it with users.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a photo editing app for desktop. Users often work with complex, layered images. Should I offer an 'outline' view like Adobe Illustrator?

Yes, consider offering an 'outline' view. Adobe Illustrator uses this mode to show graphic objects in a document, which is most useful when dealing with a lot of complex and layered objects. This view puts less demand on the computer processor, significantly speeding up work, especially for performance-intensive tasks.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My presentation software allows users to edit one slide at a time. Sometimes, they need to see all their slides together. Should I include a view like Apple Keynote's 'virtual table' layout?

Yes, offering a view where all slides are laid out on a 'virtual table' can be very beneficial, similar to how Apple Keynote handles this. While users normally edit one slide at a time, this alternative view allows them to see all their slides collectively, which can aid in organizing and managing the overall presentation structure.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a photo editing app for mobile. Users need to be able to apply filters and also adjust specific parameters like brightness and contrast. It's hard to fit both on one screen without making it cluttered.

Consider using Alternative Views. You can design one view for applying filters and another for detailed parameter adjustments. This allows users to choose the perspective they need for a specific task. Present these as alternatives within the same screen, perhaps using icons to switch between them. Ensure that when switching, the app preserves the user's current state, like any adjustments already made, so they don't lose their work.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We're building a web-based project management tool. Some users want a high-level overview of all tasks with deadlines, while others need to see detailed notes and sub-tasks for each item. How can I accommodate both needs?

Alternative Views would be beneficial here. Design a primary view that shows a structural overview of tasks, perhaps with editing handles or markers. Then, create a separate view that an end-user might see, focusing on the final presentation of information. You can also offer different ways of rendering lists, like a grid or a tree table, to cater to different preferences for viewing task information. Make sure there's an easy way to switch back to the default view and that the user's progress or selections are maintained across views.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My e-commerce website needs a print-friendly version. The current design has a lot of navigation and promotional banners that aren't relevant for printing.

You should implement Alternative Views for your website's print function. Design a print-specific view that strips down the interface by removing secondary content, shrinking or eliminating images, and cutting out all navigation but the most basic. You can implement this using alternative CSS pages. Ensure there's a clear switch for this mode on the main interface, and that users can easily switch back to the standard view. Remembering the user's choice (e.g., via cookies) can also enhance the experience.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a real estate app for iOS and need to decide how to present search results. Should I show them a map or a list, or both?

For your iOS real estate app, consider offering both a map view and a list view of query results. Users will need a way to toggle between these views. On desktop, displaying these modes side-by-side is an option. In the list view, using photographs of properties can support rapid scanning, allowing users to quickly identify properties they find most attractive.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My team is building a desktop application for searching rentals. We're debating how to show the search results. What's a good approach?

For your desktop rental search application, you can allow users to view the map and list modes side by side. This approach enables users to see spatial information alongside property details. When presenting results in a list view, incorporating photographs of the properties is beneficial. This visual aid helps users rapidly scan through numerous choices and focus on properties they deem most appealing.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a dashboard for a financial advisor to monitor multiple client portfolios on a web app. Should I use tabs or a side-by-side view?

Consider a multipanel or multistream approach, similar to TweetDeck or Hootsuite. This allows users to see multiple feeds or data points simultaneously, side by side. For your financial advisor, this means they could view several client portfolios at once, maintaining the spirit of a live stream by keeping all updates visible. If these were in different tabs or windows, the advisor wouldn't be able to see all the activity as it happens, which is crucial for monitoring multiple accounts effectively.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My app helps users track multiple social media accounts. Is it better to have users click through tabs to see each account's feed, or show them all at once?

A multipanel or multistream approach, as seen in TweetDeck and Hootsuite, is beneficial here. This pattern allows users to see multiple streams, like different social media feeds, at once. By displaying them side by side, you maintain the visibility of all updates as they happen. If you were to hide these behind navigation tabs, the user would only be able to view one at a time, missing the real-time overview that a multipanel view provides.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a code editor for web developers. They need to see their code, the live preview of the website, and maybe some documentation all at once. What pattern should I use?

You should consider the 'Many Workspaces' pattern. This pattern is ideal for applications where users need to view or edit multiple content items simultaneously, such as code files, output previews, or documentation. It allows users to have different views or task 'modes' available at the same time. You can implement this using tabs, separate OS windows, or columns/panels within a window. For complex content like development environments, using multiple panels or windows is common, allowing users to see various code files, command windows, and output logs at once. This directly supports multitasking and allows for side-by-side comparisons, which can aid in understanding and learning.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My team is building a photo editing app. Users often want to compare their current edit with the original image, or perhaps compare two different edited versions. How can we best support this?

The 'Many Workspaces' pattern can be very beneficial here. It allows users to view multiple pages, projects, files, or contexts at a time, and users might have the option to place these workspaces side by side. This directly supports side-by-side comparisons between two or more items without requiring users to laboriously switch context. Implementing this with split windows, where users can adjust the splitters interactively, would allow for easy comparison of images. This pattern supports 'Safe Exploration' because there's no cost in opening an additional workspace while leaving the original one intact.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We're making a news aggregator app. Users will see a main feed, but some might want to follow specific topics or search results in separate views. Should they be able to see these different feeds at the same time?

Yes, for a news aggregator where users might want to monitor activity across a large number of real-time feeds, the 'Many Workspaces' pattern is relevant. While a primary news stream might not inherently need multiple workspaces, you can support users who want to see filtered streams side by side. For instance, some Twitter applications show a search-based feed, a custom list feed, and a popular retweets feed concurrently. This pattern directly supports multitasking, allowing users to switch between different tasks or monitor multiple feeds simultaneously. Consider using columns or panels within a window to display these different streams.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a desktop application for photo editing. Users are confused about the purpose of the different toolbars. What kind of help can I provide?

To help users understand the toolbars in your desktop photo editing application, you should provide inline copy. This includes meaningful headlines and subheaders to label the toolbars and potentially instructions directly on the screen to clarify their purpose. Additionally, consider using tooltips, which are brief descriptions that display when a user hovers over a specific component within the toolbar. This inline and tooltip approach provides immediate assistance by communicating what the user is looking at and the purpose of each component.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My new mobile app has a complex feature for scheduling appointments, and users are struggling to figure it out on their first try. What's the best way to onboard them?

For your mobile app with a complex appointment scheduling feature, consider implementing guided tours. These are step-by-step walkthroughs, often appearing as pop-ups or pointers layered on top of the application, that can take users through the process. You can trigger these tours when a user first accesses the app or when they select a 'show me how' option. This method provides a structured way to train users and help them complete a process in a stepwise fashion, especially useful for complicated features.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We're building a web-based project management tool and want to ensure users can easily find answers to common questions about features like task assignment and deadline setting. What should we include?

To address common questions in your web-based project management tool, you should implement a full help system. This system can be embedded within the app or hosted separately and should include descriptions of features, FAQs, and how-to guides. For features like task assignment and deadline setting, this comprehensive approach provides users with detailed answers and training materials, reinforcing how to use the application effectively.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a new mobile app for booking appointments. How can I help users learn how to use it without them having to call support?

Publishing instructional and reference content directly within your app is a longstanding best practice. This provides users with a place to turn to answer their questions on their own, without having to initiate a customer service request or break from their task. New users can self-train with such materials, which builds customer confidence and satisfaction. Consider providing links to a help website or embedding a help system directly in the application, similar to how Adobe Photoshop and Microsoft Excel offer help resources.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My users are struggling with a complex feature in our desktop software. What's the best way to provide them with quick help so they don't get stuck?

You should provide a help system directly within the software platform. This is a significant benefit because users can access the information they need on their own, without interrupting their workflow to contact support. It also serves as a refresher for existing users. Microsoft Excel, for example, offers its full help system in a separate application window that can be opened while the main application is still in use, allowing users to reference information without leaving their current task.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a mobile app for tracking personal finances. What are some lightweight ways to provide help to users who are new to budgeting?

For your mobile finance app, consider using meaningful on-screen copy like descriptive headers and instructions directly on the budgeting pages. Prompt text within form fields, such as for entering income or expenses, can also guide users. Since mobile doesn't have hover states like desktop, tooltips need to be accessed via a tap, perhaps on an info icon next to a feature, to avoid irritation.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We have a complex web-based project management tool. Some users are experts, but many are beginners. How can we support both ends of the spectrum with help?

To support both beginners and experts in your web-based project management tool, use a mixture of lightweight and heavyweight help techniques. For immediate, task-focused help, implement on-screen copy, prompt text in form fields, and tooltips for non-evident interface features. For users who want to learn in more detail, offer longer help texts within Collapsible Panels or a separate online user guide. This layered approach caters to varying learning styles and commitment levels.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My company is launching a new SaaS product. We want to build a community around it. What's the best way to encourage users to help each other?

To foster a user community for your SaaS product, consider creating and moderating your own online user community. This encourages users to help one another, which can spread usage and create a culture that supports platform growth. While communities can form organically on social media, a company-hosted community ensures quality discussions and a more controlled environment for this advanced support technique.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a new photo editing app for desktop. Some of our tools are just icons. How can I help users understand what they do without cluttering the interface?

You can implement in-screen help, specifically tooltips. When a user hovers their mouse over an icon, it can change its background color and then display a tooltip that explains the tool's function. This approach enables learning through exploration, as seen in Adobe Photoshop. Ensure that these tooltips appear contextually to aid understanding.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: For our new project management tool on desktop, we have a lot of features. We want to onboard new users effectively. What are some ways to introduce them to the application?

Consider using introductory material like static introductory screens or guided tours. These can help orient new users to their first steps in the application. It's also advisable to offer a toggle switch to turn off these introductions, as users may eventually find them less useful, and provide a way to access them again later if needed.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm working on a desktop application and want to provide help. What are the different types of help resources I should consider?

You should consider several types of help resources. Introductory material such as static introductory screens, guided tours, or videos can orient new users. Help can also be shown in a separate window, often in HTML format, which can be reached via menu items or help buttons. For heavily used software, online community support can be a valuable resource. Additionally, in-screen help like labels and tooltips are crucial for contextual assistance.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing an onboarding flow for a new SaaS product. What's a good way to guide new users through the interface?

You can create step-by-step guides that act as a tour for new users. These guides often appear as an overlay on top of the standard interface or as a series of pop-ups that highlight key screen components. This approach prompts users to focus on one step or interface component at a time, promoting better learning and allowing them to proceed at their own pace while still covering important features.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We're launching a new feature in our mobile app and want to make sure users understand how to use it. What kind of in-app guidance can we provide?

Consider implementing pop-up user guides, similar to how Pendo offers them. These can be used as part of a task walk-through, highlighting the immediate next step a user needs to take, such as going to 'Settings'. This method provides clear, contextual guidance directly within the user's workflow.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: Our product is quite complex, and users often get stuck. How can we support them beyond just in-app help?

You can foster an online community where users can seek advice from each other. This extends the product's UX beyond the software itself. By creating a space like a user forum, you enable users to share knowledge, ask questions, and provide answers, which can be a huge advantage for the product, especially if users are deeply invested in it.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a community forum for a niche hobby. We have a lot of user-generated posts. Should I implement tagging?

Yes, you should enable tagging. Tags act as a user-generated classification system, allowing users to categorize and search content by topics of interest. This can grow into a homegrown topic navigation system that complements your formal IA, helping users discover relevant content more easily and increasing their investment in your platform.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: For our news app, we have a lot of articles. How can users find content related to specific events quickly?

You can allow users to tag posts, or you can add tags yourself. Tags provide a topic-based, faceted classification and navigation system. When users can search and browse content by these category descriptors, they can find information relevant to their interests at the moment, which promotes increased usage of your app.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: On our social media platform, users are already using '#' before keywords. What's the benefit of this?

The use of the hash symbol '#' before a word, creating a 'hashtag,' is a widely adopted convention for marking content as a tag. This allows users to link from a single post to a feed of other posts marked with the same hashtag. This is a powerful mechanism for information to spread rapidly and enables users to discover content from co-users marked with the same tag.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm building a community app for gardeners. Should I let users tag their posts about plants?

Yes, allowing users to add tags to their posts can help create an organic organization scheme for your app. This crowdsourced approach can be more cost-effective than building your own structure. Tags can help users find content related to their interests, increasing engagement. Ensure your search function can query tagged content and that selecting a tag generates a search results page.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: On my photo-sharing app, users are uploading lots of pictures of their pets. How can I organize these photos effectively without me having to manually categorize them?

You can enable users to tag their photos with keywords related to the content, such as 'dog' or 'cat.' This crowdsources an organization scheme. Over time, a user-centered structure can emerge. Make sure that tags are formatted as links, and selecting a tag leads to a search results page displaying all photos with that specific tag. This allows users to easily find related content.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We have a forum where developers discuss coding problems. How can we make it easier for users to find solutions to specific issues?

Implement a tagging system where users can add keywords to their discussion posts. This allows for a robust, crowd-sourced tag/topic system for finding information, similar to how Stack Overflow operates. Ensure that selecting a tag automatically generates a search, displaying relevant or recent content tagged with that term. Displaying related tags can also create a rich browsing ecosystem, helping users discover relevant content.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm building a blog for a local news site on WordPress. I want to help readers discover more content. Should I use tags?

Yes, using tags on your WordPress blog can help readers discover more content. As seen with Texas Monthly, tags displayed with articles link to other posts within the WordPress system. When a user clicks a tag like 'Travel,' they are shown a list of other travel articles. This practice promotes increased time on the site, site circulation, and reader engagement.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: On my company's website, which uses WordPress, we have many articles about different product features. How can I help users find related articles about specific features?

You can use tags to help users find related articles about specific features on your WordPress site. By adding tags to your articles, you create links to other posts within the WordPress system. For instance, if a user is reading about a specific product feature and clicks on a tag related to it, they will be directed to a page listing other articles covering that same feature. This approach is effective in increasing user engagement and time spent on the site.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a mobile app for users to save recipes. They'll be saving lots of recipes from different websites. How can I help them organize these recipes so they can find them later?

You can implement a user-generated tagging system, similar to Evernote's. When users save a recipe, allow them to add their own tags. This helps with categorizing and finding similar recipes later. You should also provide a tag index screen where users can see all existing tags and how many recipes are associated with each one. Selecting a tag should then display a list of all recipes with that tag.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My team is building a desktop application for researchers to store and categorize scientific papers. We have a lot of papers coming in. What's a good way for users to manage this large volume of information?

Consider implementing a user-generated tag system. This allows users to add their own tags when saving papers, which aids in categorizing and later finding related documents. A tag index screen would also be beneficial, showing users the available tags and the count of papers for each. This, combined with free-text searching, creates a powerful method for managing large amounts of disparate information, as seen in Evernote.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a mobile app for booking flights. Users often get lost between the search results and the booking details. What can I do to help them know where they are?

To help users know where they are, use signposts. Features like page titles, selection indicators, and breadcrumbs can inform users of their current location within the app. This helps them stay 'found' and plan their next steps.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My e-commerce website has many product categories. Users complain they can't find what they're looking for. How can I improve their ability to navigate?

Improve navigation by providing clear, unambiguous labels for your product categories. These labels should anticipate what users are looking for and instruct them where to go. Ensure these labels are placed where users expect them, and that they are never left at a decision point without guidance. This is key for wayfinding.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We have a complex web application with many tools. Users find it overwhelming. What's the core principle for navigation design?

The core principle for navigation design is that 'less is better.' While you may need to group content onto different pages or put lesser-used tools on separate screens, ensure the 'distances' users must travel remain short. The purpose of navigation is to help users understand the information space, available tools, how content is structured, where they are, and where they can go.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a mobile app for a local library. Users need to find books easily. How should I organize the book catalog?

To organize your library's book catalog, focus on creating an 'evergreen' information architecture (IA) that can accommodate new books over time. Base your organization and labeling schemes on the users' vocabulary and mental model of how they think about books. Consider using mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive categorization, chunking content, and developing a system of screen types based on usage. Search, browse, and navigation systems will be necessary for users to explore this IA effectively.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My company is launching a new project management tool. We have a lot of features and documents. How can I make sure users can find what they need?

For your project management tool, ensure the information architecture (IA) is designed to be 'evergreen,' meaning it can grow and adapt without causing confusion. Use commonly understood organization methods and chunk up content to make it digestible. Develop a system of screen types based on how users interact with the tool. Employ patterns like search, tags, and cross-links as ways for users to explore the information space and fully learn the IA.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a mobile app for a news aggregator. Where should I put the main navigation links that users will access on every screen?

For global navigation that appears on every main screen in a mobile app, you have two common approaches. One is a navigation bar that stays at the bottom of the screen. The other is a 'hamburger' menu, represented by three horizontal lines or dots, which, when tapped, reveals the navigation choices. These are standard patterns users are likely familiar with for navigating the formal structure of a site or app.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: On our e-commerce website, we have a user profile section. Where do users typically expect to find links for things like account settings and logout?

Users commonly look for utility navigation, which includes links related to non-content aspects like sign-in, help, and settings, in the upper-right corner of a website. If your visitors are typically signed-in members, placing these links there, perhaps indicated by an avatar icon, is a good practice. This is where users expect to find tools related to their presence on the site, such as account settings and logout.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm writing an article about a specific historical event. How can I help readers discover other related content on my blog?

You can use associative navigation, such as a 'Related Articles' section or panel, often placed in a sidebar or footer. This is a common way to link content thematically. Additionally, tags, whether user-defined or system-defined, can support associative navigation and help users find related articles or links, especially if your site has a large amount of content.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a mobile app for booking appointments. Users often need to book a follow-up appointment right after their current one. How should I structure the navigation to make this quick?

To minimize the cognitive cost for users booking a follow-up, keep the distances short by making this a frequent action with direct access. Elevate this frequent action to the top level of your navigation structure so users can access it directly without navigating through multiple category or subcategory menus. This flattens the site hierarchy and reduces the number of taps or clicks needed to complete the task.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We're building a news website and want to help users discover more content. Should we use tags or a deep, hierarchical menu structure?

To help users discover more content and reduce cognitive load, consider using tags at the end of articles. Each tag can serve as a link to a set of articles sharing that tag, offering a flatter navigation structure. This approach is generally better than a deep, hierarchical menu structure, as it minimizes the number of levels users need to browse and reduces the effort required to find related content. This also helps avoid presenting users with too many navigation options at the top level, which can increase cognitive cost.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: For our e-commerce site, we have many product categories. Should we put all of them in the main navigation, or just a few top-level ones?

To optimize navigation and reduce cognitive load, it's better to have more selections at the first, topmost level. This means making the site structure as flat as possible. Instead of having just a few top-level navigation items that require users to navigate through many category and subcategory menus, put access to more screens directly in the global navigation. This keeps distances short and reduces the number of taps or clicks required to get from point A to point B.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a new suite of enterprise web applications for our company. Users need to access all of them easily after logging in once. What's a good way to structure this?

Your enterprise web applications can be tied together at the top behind a single sign-on and a single point of access. This way, users can access each of the applications and manage their account settings from one place, even if the apps are separate experiences or were acquired at different times.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We're building an e-commerce checkout flow. Users add items to their cart and then proceed to pay. How should I guide them through this?

You can use a stepwise navigation model for your e-commerce purchase flow. Design a clear path from the product page to the shopping cart, then through the checkout process (which might involve multiple screens), and finally to a confirmation of the completed transaction. This guides the user step by step through a prescribed sequence.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: For our online clothing subscription service, we need to collect customer preferences like style and budget before they start. What's a good approach for this initial step?

A questionnaire or online survey can serve as the first step in your customer onboarding process. This allows the customer to step through a series of questions that establish their preferences, such as style, budget, sizes, and frequency of delivery, guiding them through the initial setup.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a mobile app for a small bakery. It has a home screen, and then individual pages for each type of pastry (cakes, cookies, bread). I want users to be able to easily see all the pastry types from any pastry page. What's the best way to structure this?

Consider a **fully connected** navigational model for your bakery app. This means that from the home screen and every pastry page, users should be able to jump to any other pastry page with a single action. A global navigation feature, like a top menu listing all pastry categories, would achieve this. This ensures users can easily explore all offerings without getting lost.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My website has a main product catalog page, and then sub-pages for each individual product. Users can get from the catalog to a product page easily, but if they want to compare two products, they have to go back to the catalog first. Is there a way to make this easier?

Your current structure sounds like a **multilevel or tree** model, where subpages are connected only among themselves. To improve comparison, you could convert this to a **fully connected** model. This might involve using patterns like a 'Fat Menus' (drop-down menus) or a 'Sitemap Footer' that allows users to jump between any product subpages directly, rather than always returning to the main catalog.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We have a complex enterprise software with many features. The main dashboard links to all major modules. However, within each module, there are many detailed screens. Users often complain they can't easily navigate between screens in different modules. What's the issue here?

This sounds like a **multilevel or tree** navigation where your main pages (modules) are fully connected, but the subpages (detailed screens within modules) are only connected among themselves. Users need multiple jumps to get from a subpage in one module to a subpage in another. To make this more user-friendly, consider implementing global navigation or using patterns like 'Fat Menus' or a 'Sitemap Footer' to create a **fully connected** experience, allowing a single jump between any two arbitrary subpages.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a photo editing app like Photoshop. Users will have tons of tools. How should I organize them?

For applications with many tools, like Photoshop, consider making them accessible via menus, toolbars, and palettes. Tools that don't immediately affect the work can be placed in Modal Panels or accessed through step-by-step progressions. The key is that users generally know where they are, but the challenge is helping them find the specific tools they need among the many available.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We're building a full-screen slideshow feature. What navigation is essential, and what should we avoid?

During a full-screen slideshow, users are focused on the content. Avoid complex global navigation menus that take up space and increase cognitive load. Instead, provide only essential controls like Back/Next buttons and an Escape Hatch. This focused navigation allows users to concentrate on the slideshow itself.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: Our app has a lot of content and users sometimes get lost. What patterns can help them know where they are and where they're going?

To help users orient themselves, consider using 'You are here' signposts. Patterns like Progress Indicators, Breadcrumbs, and Annotated Scroll Bars can serve this purpose. Progress Indicators, Breadcrumbs, and Annotated Scroll Bars can also act as interactive maps of the content, helping users understand their location and plan their next steps.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a landing page for a new car dealership app. I want to offer users multiple ways to explore the cars. What's a good pattern for this?

Consider using a pattern similar to Tesla's landing page. They offer multiple entry points, represented by their car models. The key is to provide clear options for users to choose from, such as 'Customize your own' or 'Browse available cars now.' This allows users to select the path that best suits their immediate goal.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My app's main screen needs to present a lot of different content categories. How should I structure it?

A 'Menu Page' pattern, like the one seen on Craigslist, could be effective. This pattern involves filling the page with a list of links to your content-rich pages. Ensure you provide enough information about each link so users can make informed choices, and avoid cluttering the page with other significant content.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a new mobile app for booking local dog walkers. We have a lot of features, but I'm worried first-time users will be overwhelmed. What's the best way to guide them?

To help first-time users, present a few main entry points into your app. These should be task-oriented, like 'Book a Walk' or 'Find a Walker,' using clear calls to action. This removes the burden of learning and allows users to confidently choose an option, contributing to immediate gratification. Avoid overwhelming them with too much information or too many choices upfront.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My company's internal dashboard has tons of data and tools. When new employees log in, they often look lost. How can I make it more self-explanatory?

For new employees, list a few clear options for getting started on the dashboard. These entry points should act as 'doors' into the main content, guiding them gently. Phrase these options with language they can understand, not internal jargon. Visually emphasize these entry points proportionally to their importance, making them more prominent than other navigation links.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We're launching a website for a historical society. Many visitors are infrequent and might not know where to begin. Should I just list all our archives?

Instead of listing all archives, which might be overwhelming, present a few main entry points. Make them task-oriented, such as 'Explore Our Collections' or 'Learn About Our History,' using clear calls to action. This helps infrequent users know where to start and reduces the burden of learning the site. Ensure these entry points cover the most common reasons users visit.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a landing page for a new photo editing app on mobile. How can I guide first-time users to the most important features?

You can orient new users to the possibilities for action by providing different entry points. For example, Adobe Illustrator uses a startup dialog with major actions like 'Create New' and 'Open.' Consider offering a clear call to action, similar to Spotify's landing page, to guide users toward their primary goals. You might also present options with varying levels of detail, from bold buttons for experienced users to more visual and explanatory approaches for those less familiar.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My company's website needs a better 'front door.' We offer a complex B2B software, and potential clients are often unsure if it's right for them. What's a good pattern?

To address potential buyer uncertainty and differentiate your product, use your website's entry point to make it easy for potential customers to move toward understanding and engagement. Like Prezi, which uses its front door to signal 'Come in and check it out,' your landing page should convey an invitation to explore. This can help alleviate the biggest questions in the minds of potential buyers by demonstrating the value and innovation of your software.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a desktop application and want to help users who haven't used it before. What's a good way to introduce them to the main functions?

A startup dialog can effectively orient new or infrequent users to the possibilities for action. For instance, Adobe Illustrator presents major actions like creating something new or opening an existing document. It offers different entry points: boldly marked buttons like 'Create New' and 'Open' for more experienced users, and a more visual, explanatory approach with choices representing likely scenarios for less confident users. Including a 'Recent' section with thumbnails of recently opened files can also aid recognition and recall.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a website for a large university. Users will land on a page listing all academic departments. What's the best way to organize these links?

Organize the links on your academic departments page as a list, similar to the University of California, Berkeley's approach. The goal is to move visitors quickly to a page that answers their specific need, such as finding a particular department or resource. Avoid providing general information about the university on this page, as users arriving here are likely searching for something well-defined.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: Our city government website needs to provide access to many different departments and services. What's a good pattern for this?

Consider a portal approach for your city government website, like SF.gov. This pattern is effective for large organizations with many departments and services. It brings together links to all these areas, assuming the majority of users are searching for a specific department or service within the governmental structure.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a resource hub for our company's internal design team. Should I use a page that just lists articles, or something more visual?

Consider using a Menu Page, like the one on the AIGA website. This pattern presents articles with thumbnail images and summary text. This rich format provides enough context for users to decide if they want to invest time in clicking through to the full article, making the page intriguing on its own.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My team is building a new section for our design blog. We want to make sure users can easily browse and decide which articles to read. What's a good pattern for this?

A Menu Page is a suitable pattern for this. The AIGA website uses this approach by displaying articles with thumbnail images and summary text. This rich format offers sufficient context, allowing users to make an informed decision about which articles to explore further, thus hooking their interest.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We're redesigning the landing page for our upcoming exhibit. We have a lot of visual content. What's a good way to present it to users?

You could adapt the Menu Page pattern, similar to how the Museum of Modern Art presents its PS1 exhibit menu. They use large images and small text, which can be effective for visually rich content, allowing users to quickly grasp the essence of each item.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a mobile app for a local library. It has a lot of sections like 'Browse Fiction', 'Browse Non-Fiction', 'Events', 'Services', 'About Us', etc. I want to make sure users can easily find what they're looking for. What kind of page should I use?

For a mobile app with many navigation options, a Menu Page is a principal tool. Keep list labels short, make tap targets large enough, and avoid overly deep hierarchies. It's easy for these pages to become overwhelming, so consider using them for less frequently accessed sections or reference pages. Ensure you align, group, and label content for easier comprehension.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We're building a website for a new startup that explains its innovative tech. We have a lot of information to convey about our value proposition. Should we use a page that's just a list of links to different product features?

A pure Menu Page might not be the best choice if your site needs to explain its value and purpose. Such a page is best when visitors know what the site is about, what they came for, and want to find it quickly without distractions. If you need to hook visitors and convey information, consider using some page space for promotional items or other interesting content instead of a dense menu.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf
