Pare down the app to highlight the main functionality, like Music Memos does for recording. Offering a graceful animation to indicate the action is taking place, such as a recording in progress, can also enhance the user experience.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a mobile app for a photo gallery. What's a good way to lay out the main content on the screen?

Consider using the Vertical Stack pattern. This means ordering your content in a vertical column, allowing text to wrap and the screen to scroll. This approach maximizes flexibility for mobile screens, where side-by-side elements are less common and valuable screen space is often limited.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: For my mobile news app, I need to display a list of articles. What patterns should I consider?

Well-designed lists are crucial for mobile. Ordinary text lists are often adequate. For visual appeal and usability on mobile, consider patterns like Carousel or Thumbnail Grid. If you have a very large number of items, an Infinite List might also suit your needs.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My mobile banking app needs a persistent way for users to navigate between different sections like accounts, transfers, and profile. What are some common patterns for this?

A standard approach for organizing a mobile interface's top-level navigation is a persistent toolbar, which can be placed across the top or bottom of each screen. Other common methods include using tabs or full-screen menus. For mobile web screens, the Bottom Navigation pattern is often preferred to reserve top-of-screen space for content.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a news app for iOS. I want to use a filmstrip for the main navigation, with screens like 'Top Stories', 'My News', 'Popular', 'Video', and 'Live'. What should I be aware of?

When using a Filmstrip pattern for your app's main screens, be mindful that it doesn't scale well if you have too many top-level screens, as users might get irritated swiping too many times. Also, new users may not immediately understand that swiping is the primary navigation method. To address this lack of transparency, consider using a dot indicator, similar to how the iPhone's Weather app indicates multiple screens and allows swiping between them.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My mobile app needs to display weather for multiple locations. I'm thinking of using a filmstrip for the main navigation. Is this a good approach?

Using a Filmstrip pattern for your mobile app's main screens, like the iPhone's Weather app does for different geographic locations, can work. However, a disadvantage is that it doesn't scale well; too many screens can lead to user irritation from excessive swiping. Additionally, a new user might not intuitively grasp that swiping is the navigation method. To improve discoverability, you could implement a dot indicator to signal the presence of multiple screens and the ability to swipe between them.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a mobile app for a news aggregator. We have a lot of articles and want users to be able to browse them easily with one hand. What layout should I consider?

Consider a vertical stack layout, similar to Salon.com. This pattern allows for flexibility in displaying content and makes it easy for users to scroll through new articles with their thumb, enabling one-handed browsing.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We're building a travel app that shows weather forecasts for different cities. Users need to compare them. Should we use a filmstrip pattern?

Yes, the filmstrip pattern is a good fit for your travel app. It allows users to swipe back and forth between conceptually parallel content, like weather forecasts for different cities, viewing one screen at a time. This pattern encourages browsing and can be a viable alternative to other navigation schemes for mobile apps.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: For our photo gallery app on mobile, we have many full-screen images. Users should be able to easily move between them. What's a good interaction pattern?

The filmstrip pattern would work well for your photo gallery app. Each image can occupy the entire screen, and users can navigate by swiping back and forth. This pattern encourages browsing and serendipity, as users swipe through images one at a time.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a mobile web app for a news site. The content is mostly text-based articles and forms for comments. What's the best way to lay out the screen?

Use a Vertical Stack pattern. This is ideal for mobile web screens with text-based content and forms, especially when working with devices of different widths. Lay out content in a scrolling vertical column, placing the most important items at the top. Ensure essential material is visible within the first 100 pixels to make the most of that precious screen real estate. Avoid stacking too many elements like tall logos or toolbars at the top, as this pushes useful content off-screen and can annoy users. For forms, place labels above their controls to save horizontal space, which is needed for text fields and choice controls. Be cautious about placing buttons side-by-side; only do this if you're certain their combined width won't exceed the screen, especially if button text might be localized or enlarged.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We have a mobile web app with a lot of user-submitted content, like product reviews and forum posts. Users are complaining about slow loading times when navigating between pages. What pattern should we consider?

A Vertical Stack pattern is applicable here. Since going from one screen to another on the web involves download time, a scrolling vertical layout is preferable to interminable waits. Structure your content into a single, scrollable screen rather than breaking it into multiple pages that require downloads. This allows users to access content by scrolling vertically, which is generally better than waiting for new pages to load.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My mobile web app has a lot of form fields and buttons. How can I make sure they fit on smaller screens?

When using a Vertical Stack, prioritize screen real estate. Place form labels above their respective controls to conserve horizontal space, as you'll need width for text fields and choice controls. If you're considering placing buttons side-by-side, ensure their total width will never exceed the visible screen, especially if the button text could be subject to localization or font enlargements. It's also crucial to ensure your design degrades gracefully when screen width is reduced to a realistic minimum.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a mobile app for watching videos, similar to Netflix. How can I make sure the video content is the main focus but users can still access controls like play/pause?

You should use Touch Tools. This pattern allows you to show controls in small, dynamic overlays when the user interacts with the screen, like tapping. This way, the content can dominate the experience most of the time, and the user isn’t distracted by controls taking up space and attention. The tools should appear when the user touches the screen and then disappear after a few seconds of nonuse, or if they tap outside the tool area, to avoid annoyance.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: For my photo gallery app on mobile, I want the images to fill the screen. How do I let users navigate between photos or access editing tools without them being permanently visible?

Implement Touch Tools. This is ideal for immersive, full-screen experiences like photo galleries. The unadorned content should use the full screen, and when the user touches the screen, you can show navigation or editing tools in a small, translucent overlay. This makes the tools look ephemeral and ensures they don't permanently obscure the images. Remember to remove them after a few seconds of nonuse or when the user taps elsewhere to avoid clutter.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm building a mobile game where the gameplay needs to be front and center. How can I provide in-game controls that don't get in the way?

Consider using Touch Tools for your mobile game. Since gameplay is an immersive experience, you'll want the content to dominate. When the user touches the screen, you can display necessary game controls in small, translucent overlays. This pattern ensures the controls are available when needed but don't distract from the main gameplay. The tools should disappear after a period of inactivity or when the user taps outside their bounds, keeping the focus on the game itself.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a mobile e-commerce app and need to decide where to place the main navigation links. We want to prioritize showing new products on the home screen.

Place the global navigation at the bottom of the screen, as this is a pattern called Bottom Navigation. This is useful when you need to show global navigation links that are low-priority paths for many users, and your highest priority on the front screen is to show fresh content. The top of a mobile home screen is precious real estate, so dedicating it to content is key. Users can easily scroll to the bottom to find navigational links. Make these links easy to tap, stretch them across the full width, and ensure the text is large and readable.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: For our news app on mobile, should I put the main sections like 'World', 'Politics', and 'Tech' at the top or bottom of the screen?

Consider using Bottom Navigation and placing these main sections at the bottom of the screen. This pattern is recommended when you have global navigation links that are not the primary focus for most users on the home screen, allowing you to prioritize content at the top. Users can readily scroll down to access these navigation items. Ensure the navigation items are large, readable, and easy to tap, potentially spanning the full width of the screen.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a mobile website for a retail store. We have a few key sections like 'Shop', 'Account', and 'Help'. Where's the best place for these?

Implement a Bottom Navigation pattern by placing these key sections at the bottom of the screen. This approach is effective for mobile websites where you need to display global navigation links that represent lower-priority paths for many users, thus freeing up the valuable top real estate for content. Users can easily scroll to the bottom to find these links. Make sure the links are easily tappable, with large, readable text, and consider making them span the full width of the mobile screen for better usability.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a news app for mobile. How can I make my article list more engaging?

To make your article list more engaging on mobile, use thumbnail images next to the text. This pattern helps people scan and parse the list quickly by improving visual differentiation among items. Many news sites use this to make their lists look more appealing and finished than those with only titles. Don't be afraid to use bright or saturated colors, as small screens can handle strong colors better than large screens.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: For my mobile app's list of products, should I just show the name and a short description, or can I add images?

You should definitely add thumbnail images next to the product names and descriptions. This pattern not only makes the list look more appealing and 'finished' but also helps users identify items more easily and establishes a generous height for each list item, which is beneficial for scanning on mobile. Consider using bright or saturated colors for these images, as they work well on small screens.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm building a mobile app that lists user profiles. What's a good way to visually distinguish each profile in the list view?

Place a thumbnail image next to the profile information. This pattern helps improve visual differentiation, making it easier for users to scan and parse the list quickly. In addition to profile pictures, you can include other visual markers like social presence icons. Bright or saturated colors can be effective here, as small screens handle strong colors better than large ones.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a mobile app for a news aggregator. Users will be scrolling through hundreds of articles. Should I load them all at once?

No, you should not load all articles at once. To avoid forcing users to wait for a long initial screen load, truncate the list to a reasonable length for the initial display. At the bottom of the screen, provide a button to 'load more items,' letting the user know how many will be loaded. This approach, often called lazy loading, ensures a fast initial experience and allows users to load more content only when they indicate they need it by scrolling.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My e-commerce app has a very long list of products. How can I make sure users don't get frustrated waiting for the page to load?

For long lists of products on your e-commerce app, use lazy loading. Initially, display only a screenful or two of items. This ensures a fast initial load, allowing users to see useful content quickly. As the user scrolls to the bottom of the visible list, you can either present a button to load more, or silently start loading the next chunk of items and append them to the visible list. This prevents long waits and keeps the user engaged by not forcing them to context-shift to a new screen for more items.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm building a social media feed for a mobile app. Users expect to see new posts as they scroll. What's the best way to handle this?

For a social media feed on a mobile app, an infinite scroll pattern is highly effective. Load the first several screens of posts initially, and then as the user scrolls down, silently load and append subsequent posts. This 'lazy loading' approach ensures that new content is readily available without requiring the user to explicitly request it, and it keeps the user from having to navigate to a new screen to see more updates.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm building a real estate app on mobile and the property listings take a few seconds to load after a user searches. What should I show them?

Use an infinite loop or image placeholders at the top of the screen to indicate that the screen is loading. This lets the user know that something is happening and that the system is working to provide the requested information.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My music app needs to show the user that it's actively listening to find a song. What kind of visual feedback can I provide?

Implement a beautiful and simple animation to indicate that the system is listening and searching for a match. This provides clear feedback to the user about the system's current state and ongoing process.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: When a user installs a new app on their phone, how can I show them the download progress?
Use a miniature circular progress indicator over the app's icon. This is a clear and easily understood visual cue that shows the user how far the download has progressed.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a mobile app that displays a list of articles, each with a small image. I want users to be able to tap on an article to read it. What pattern should I use?

You should use the Collections and Cards pattern. This pattern is ideal for displaying lists of complex content like articles, especially when they have associated images. It invites users to click or tap on items to view them in more detail. Ensure your design clearly presents the thumbnail images alongside the article titles to make the list scannable and engaging.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My e-commerce app needs to show a list of products, and each product has an image and some basic info. Users should be able to tap on a product to see its details. What's the best way to structure this?

The Collections and Cards pattern is well-suited for your e-commerce app. Cards are specifically mentioned as being used frequently on e-commerce sites and contain content and functionality. This pattern will allow you to present product images and information in a way that invites users to tap and explore further.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a mobile app for ordering food. The buttons for adding items to the cart are currently just text. What's the best way to make sure users don't accidentally miss them?

To ensure users can easily tap your 'add to cart' buttons, you should provide generous borders around them. This means adding ample margin and whitespace. While the button might look the same size visually, making the surrounding whitespace tappable can increase its sensitivity for fingertips. This is especially important for text-only buttons, as they need to be tall enough to be manipulated successfully by fingers, particularly when users might be in challenging conditions like bad light or on the move.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: On our new e-reader app, we have a list of book titles that users can tap to open. Some users are complaining they're having trouble selecting the right one. How can we improve this?

For your list of book titles, implement generous borders by surrounding each tappable link with sufficient margin, border, and whitespace. This creates a larger hit target for fingertips. The goal is to make these touch targets large enough on the physical device to be manipulated easily, considering that fingers vary in size and users may have less precise control or be distracted. For reference, consider dimensions like 48x48 dp for Android or 44x44 pt for iOS.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We're designing a photo editing app for tablets. The toolbar has small icons that users need to tap quickly. What's the best practice for making these icons easy to hit?

Even for icons, it's crucial to provide generous borders by adding ample margin and whitespace around each tappable control. This ensures that the touch targets are large enough for fingers to tap successfully. While icons might seem inherently tappable, increasing the tappable area, perhaps by including the surrounding whitespace, will improve usability. This is particularly important on touch screens where precision can be a challenge, and users might be operating in less-than-ideal conditions.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm building a mobile app for a recipe site. When a user taps to view a recipe, it can take a few seconds to load the ingredients and instructions. What should I show them while they wait?

You should use a lightweight animated progress indicator to show that something is happening. This is useful because loading content, especially over mobile connections, can be slow and erratic. Showing a progress indicator reassures the user that their action has registered and that the content is on its way. Ideally, show as much of the screen as can be loaded quickly, and place the indicator where the content will appear. This can make the wait feel shorter and even be a brand-building moment if done well.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My e-commerce app has a feature where users can add items to their cart, and the cart count updates. Sometimes, the cart update takes a moment to reflect on the screen. How can I handle this delay?

When a user initiates an action that causes part of the screen to be reloaded, like updating a cart count, you should show a progress indicator in situ on the screen. This tells the user that the action is being processed and reassures them that something is happening. It's important to show this indicator where the change will occur, making the interaction feel more immediate and less frustrating.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a news app for mobile. When a user opens an article, it sometimes takes a while for all the images to load. What's the best practice here?

For elements that take a long time to load, such as graphics or videos within an article, display a lightweight animated progress indicator in the space where the graphic will eventually appear. This is a microinteraction that informs the user that content is loading. While you should show as much of the screen as possible that loads quickly, the indicator manages expectations for the slower-loading parts, making the overall experience feel more responsive.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a mobile banking app. What's a key consideration for integrating features like check deposit?

For your mobile banking app, consider how to integrate functionality like check deposit. Simple's banking application, for instance, invokes camera functionality to read check information. This highlights the importance of seamless integration with device capabilities to support core tasks.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My team is building a new mobile app. What's the general advice for designing for smartphones and tablets?

When designing for smartphones and tablets, remember that these interfaces are critical to user experience, not afterthoughts. Your mobile app might be the primary way users interact with your brand, so pay close attention to the small details, microinteractions, and usability within the mobile context of use.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We're developing a calendar app for mobile. What are some examples of how mobile apps integrate with other phone features?

Mobile applications often integrate with other device features to enhance functionality. Google's Calendar application, for example, integrates with the phone's maps, contact lists, and email. Consider similar integrations for your calendar app to provide a richer user experience.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm building a recipe app for iOS. Users often want to look up ingredients on a shopping list. How can I make this easier for them?

You can leverage the device's built-in functionality by making your ingredient names tappable. When a user taps an ingredient, you can direct them to another app, like a browser, to search for it. This is beneficial because users can only view one app at a time on mobile, and manually switching between apps is annoying. Automatically supporting data transfer, like launching a browser search for an ingredient, helps bridge this gap, as direct copy-pasting of small amounts of information between apps is difficult on mobile.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My travel app shows flight details, including departure and arrival airports. How can I help users easily find directions to the airport?

Since your app displays addresses (airport locations), you can make these tappable to launch the map application. This pattern is useful because users often see 'connectable' data like addresses in an app. By linking directly to the map app, you provide an intelligent path between your app and a device-based function, avoiding the annoyance of manual app switching for navigation.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a social media app where users can post updates. How can I allow them to easily share photos they've taken?

Your app can offer ways to capture images by invoking the device's camera functionality. When a user wants to post a photo, you can direct them to the camera app to take or select a picture, and then handle that data within your application. This is a good use of 'richly connected apps' because it utilizes device features that come 'for free' and supports moving data automatically, which is crucial on mobile where traditional desktop methods like copy-paste are less convenient.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a mobile app for a bookstore. Users need to browse books. What should I consider for the list of books?

For your bookstore app, consider the use cases for the book list. Will users be getting an overview of new arrivals, browsing item by item, or searching for a specific title? If they're browsing, ensure it's easy to tap a book to open its details and then return to the list or move to the next book. If they're searching, make sure the search functionality is quick and requires minimal interaction. For an overview, consider using images or careful visual organization to convey the essence of the books at a glance.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: On our e-commerce site, users can manage their wishlists. What's the best way for them to reorder items in their wishlist?

Since users own their wishlists, they should have the ability to directly manipulate the items. Allow them to rearrange items, perhaps by dragging and dropping them into a desired order. Also, consider if they need to select multiple items for bulk actions like moving or deleting. You can either use platform standards for multiple selection or provide checkboxes next to each item.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We have a long list of search results for a travel website. How can we help users find what they're looking for efficiently?

For a long list of search results, like those on a travel website, users are likely searching for specific items. Ensure they can find what they're looking for quickly with minimum clicks and scrolling. Consider implementing sorting and filtering functions based on characteristics like dates or prices to help them narrow down the results and gain insight into the data.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a mobile app for managing user-uploaded photos. Should I show a small thumbnail or the full photo in the list view?

For a mobile screen, which often has limited space, consider the 'One-Window Drilldown' pattern. This means you'd show a representation of the item, like a thumbnail, in the list and then display the full photo when the user selects it. This avoids awkward context switches but can lead to 'pogo-sticking' between screens if users frequently navigate back and forth. Showing thumbnails is a good way to present visuals associated with each item and can help users browse and find items of interest.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We're building an email client and need to display messages in a list. What's the best way to show message details?

Since email messages have a strict field-like structure (timestamp, from, subject), you can leverage this. Consider showing just the item names or representations in the list, and then displaying the full message when selected. For email, a 'Two-Panel Selector' or 'Split View' could work well, showing message details next to the list. This supports overview and browsing as everything is visible at once, maintaining context without screen reloads. Alternatively, 'List Inlay' could embed details within the list itself, expanding on selection, though this might make overview harder if many items are open.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My app shows a list of news articles. How much information should I display for each article in the list?

For a browsing-oriented experience like news articles, showing more information per item in the list can make it more interesting. Consider displaying item names, and if visuals are available, show thumbnails. You can choose to show just a representation of the item, like the headline and perhaps the first few sentences, hiding the rest until the user selects it. This balances providing enough detail for browsing with keeping the list manageable.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing an e-commerce app for mobile and need to display a lot of product images. What's the best way to show a grid of product photos?

For displaying a list of items with heavy visuals, consider using a Thumbnail Grid. This pattern presents a 2D grid of small pictures that can be visually powerful and draw attention. While text can be shown with thumbnails, the images should be the primary focus. This pattern is a generalization of the Grid of Equals. If screen space is a concern, a Carousel is an alternative, but it's linear and requires active scrolling, potentially limiting the visibility of individual items compared to a Thumbnail Grid.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My website has a very long list of articles, and I'm worried about loading times and user experience. How can I manage this long list effectively?

To manage a very long list, Pagination is a common technique. It allows you to load the list in sections, requiring the user to actively load subsequent screens. This is useful when the entire list would be too long or take too long to load initially, and when users are likely to find what they need on the first screen. Ensure your Pagination control clearly indicates the total number of screens and allows users to jump between them. An alternative for mobile is the Infinite List, where more items load as the user scrolls, keeping them on a single screen.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I have a list of user settings that are organized into categories like 'Account', 'Privacy', and 'Notifications'. Some categories have sub-categories. How should I structure this on a desktop interface?

For a list organized into categories, Titled Sections can work well for a single level of containment, separating sections with titles. If you have only a few sections and want to save space, an Accordion allows users to close sections they don't need. For two or more levels of hierarchy, such as sub-categories, basic trees are a standard solution. These typically use indented levels and icons like plus/minus signs or triangles to allow users to open and close branches of the hierarchy.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a file manager app for desktop. How should I lay out the list of folders and the details of the selected folder?

Place the selectable list of folders on the left panel and the details panel to its right. This layout takes advantage of the visual flow that users who read left-to-right languages expect. When a user selects a folder, immediately show its contents or details in the second panel. Selection should be done with a single click, but also provide a way to change selection using the arrow keys for keyboard-only usability.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: For my email client on mobile, I have a list of emails and a view for the selected email. Should the list be on top or to the side?

Consider placing the list of emails on the top and the selected email's content below it. This arrangement can work well, especially if the list benefits from being nearly as wide as the entire window. Ensure the selected email is visually obvious, perhaps by making its background or foreground color different from unselected emails.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm building a GUI builder. How should I present the properties of a selected object?

Use a two-panel layout where the selectable list (in this case, the layout canvas showing selectable objects) is on one side, and the details panel (the property editor) is next to it. This pattern has identical semantics to tabs, with one area for selectors and another for the content of the selected item. Make sure the selected object on the canvas is visually distinct.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a mobile app for a news reader. We have a list of articles, and when a user taps one, we want to show the full article content. What's the best way to handle this transition?

Use the One-Window Drilldown pattern. This pattern is ideal for mobile applications where you have a list of items, like your articles. When the user selects an article, replace the current list view with the full article content on the same screen. This approach keeps the user within a single window, making navigation simpler and more direct, as seen in the Mac Mail example on iPhone.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We're building a photo gallery app for tablets. Users will see a grid of thumbnails, and when they tap a thumbnail, we want to show the full-size image. Should we navigate to a new screen or replace the current one?

The One-Window Drilldown pattern is suitable here. For your photo gallery app, when a user selects a thumbnail, replace the grid of images with the full-size image in the same window. This pattern is effective when each item in a list has significant content, like a full-size image, and you want to maintain a focused view for the user.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a news reader app for tablets. Users will see a list of headlines on the left and the full article on the right. What's the best way to structure this?

Use a Two-Panel Selector, also known as a Split View. This pattern is ideal when you have a list of items (headlines) and each item has associated content (the full article) that the user will want to view. It allows users to see the overall structure of the list while easily browsing through items. The advantage here is reduced physical effort and cognitive load, as the user doesn't have to navigate between separate screens. The list panel acts as a 'You are here' signpost, reducing memory burden, and it's faster than loading a new screen for each article.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: For our project management tool on desktop, we have a list of projects on the left and detailed project information on the right. Users need to quickly switch between projects and see the details. What pattern should I use?

A Two-Panel Selector or Split View is appropriate for your project management tool. This pattern works well when you want users to keep the list of items (projects) in view while easily browsing through their details. The side-by-side panels reduce physical effort and cognitive load because the user can shift attention back and forth without major context switches. The visible list also reduces the user's memory burden by serving as a constant reminder of their current location within the project structure.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a photo gallery app for iPads. Users will see thumbnails on the left and the full image on the right. Is this a good use case for a split view?

Yes, a Two-Panel Selector or Split View is a good pattern for your photo gallery app on iPads. It's effective when you have a list of items (thumbnails) with associated content (full-sized images) and the display is large enough to accommodate two panels. This pattern allows users to quickly browse through images while keeping the list in view, reducing the need to remember their position in the gallery and minimizing the effort required to switch between viewing thumbnails and full images.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a mobile app for managing personal finances. Users will see a list of transactions, and when they tap one, I want to show the transaction details. What's the best way to do this?

Consider using a List Inlay pattern. This pattern shows a list of items (your transactions) as rows in a column. When a user selects an item, its details open in place within the list itself. This is useful because it allows users to see the details within the context of the overall list, which can aid understanding. It also enables users to see the details of multiple transactions at once, which is helpful for comparison. Ensure that the details panel can be easily closed, ideally with a control near where it was opened, to avoid forcing the user to scroll to the bottom of a potentially long panel.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: For our email client, we have a list of emails on the left and want to show the content of the selected email on the right. We also want users to be able to compare two emails side-by-side. What pattern should I use for the email list?

A List Inlay would be a suitable pattern for displaying your list of emails. It shows items (emails) in a column, and when an email is selected, its content (details) opens in place. This pattern is particularly strong when users need to compare two or more items, as they can view multiple email contents simultaneously. Furthermore, a List Inlay can be effectively combined with a Two-Panel Selector (which you are already using for the email list structure) to create a three-level hierarchy. This means the email list itself can use List Inlay, while the containers for those emails (like mailboxes) are in the Two-Panel Selector.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm building a news reader app where users see a list of article headlines. When they tap a headline, I want to show the full article content. The articles can be quite long. What should I do?

A List Inlay is a good pattern for this scenario. It displays your article headlines as rows in a column, and when a user selects a headline, the full article content (the item's details) opens in place within the list. This keeps the overall list structure visible while allowing users to browse through articles easily. Because the article content can be long, it's important to use a scrolled area to contain the expanding vertical structure. Also, provide a clear control to close the article details, and consider placing it near the headline that was opened to make it easy for the user to close the content without having to scroll all the way down.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a mobile app for a recipe discovery service. Users need to see a list of recipes and then tap on one to see the full details. What's the best way to handle this navigation?

Consider using the One-Window Drilldown pattern. This approach is suitable when you need the entire screen to display both the list of recipes and the details of a selected recipe. When a user selects a recipe from the list, replace the list display with the recipe's details. Ensure there's a clear 'Back' button on the item screen to return the user to the recipe list. This pattern helps prevent users from getting too deep into the UI and makes it easy to return to their starting point. Be aware that navigating between multiple recipe items can require multiple taps, as the user has to go back to the list to select another. You can mitigate this by adding 'Next' and 'Previous' links within the recipe details to allow users to step through items without returning to the list.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My team is building a news reader app for tablets. We have a list of articles, and when a user taps an article, we want to show the full content. How should we structure this flow?

The One-Window Drilldown pattern is a good fit here. You can present the list of articles, and when a user selects one, replace the list view with the full article content. This pattern gives each view the entire available space to 'spread out.' Make sure to include a 'Back' button or similar control so the user can easily return to the article list. While this pattern can lead to a 'pogo-sticking' effect if users want to compare many articles, you can improve the experience by providing 'Back' and 'Next' links within the article view to allow sequential browsing of articles.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We're designing a forum app where users see a list of conversation threads. Tapping a thread shows the posts within it. What pattern should we use?

The One-Window Drilldown pattern is well-suited for this scenario, as online forums often require the full screen width for listing conversation topics and then a separate, potentially scrolled, view for the conversations themselves. When a user selects a thread, replace the list of topics with the conversation. Provide a 'Back' button to return to the thread list. This pattern simplifies navigation by keeping the hierarchy shallow. A potential drawback is that moving between threads requires returning to the list, but you can ease this by offering 'Back' and 'Next' links to connect directly to adjacent threads.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a restaurant discovery app for mobile. Should I use borders around the cards displaying restaurant info?

Consider using cards without borders, similar to Uber Eats. This approach can effectively display key information like photos and restaurant names while maintaining a clean visual presentation. Avoid unnecessary borders that might detract from the content.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: For my photo gallery app, I need to show a grid of user photos. What's a good pattern for this?

Arrange your list of visual items into a 'small multiples' grid of photos. This pattern, as seen in the Google Photos app, is effective for presenting multiple images in an organized and easily scannable layout.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a mobile app for a local library. When a user taps on a book title in a list, I want to show them more details without navigating to a completely new screen. What pattern should I use?

Consider using a List Inlay. This pattern allows you to expand an item within the existing list to reveal more information, similar to how Apple's Voice Memos app shows playback controls when a recording is tapped. This keeps the user oriented within the list and focuses their attention on the newly revealed details without losing their place.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My e-commerce app shows a list of products. When a user taps a product, I want to show them a quick way to add it to their cart or view more details, but I don't want them to leave the product listing page. What's a good approach?

A List Inlay would be a suitable pattern here. It allows the list item to expand in situ, revealing additional options or details like adding to a cart. This is analogous to how the Transit app expands a route to show arrival times and stops. The key benefit is that everything remains in a single column, and panels open and close within the list itself, maintaining user orientation.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I have a list of tasks in my project management tool. When a user taps on a task, I want to show them an editor to update the task details directly within the list. What pattern fits this scenario?

You should look into using a List Inlay for your task list. If your application permits users to edit items, a List Inlay can be used to open an editor directly within the list. This pattern functions like an Accordion, where panels open and close in place, keeping the user focused on the task they are editing without navigating away from the overall list.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a mobile app for a recipe discovery platform. I need to display a list of recipes, and each recipe has an image, title, ingredients summary, and a 'save' button. What's the best way to present this list?

Consider using Cards for your recipe list. Cards are self-contained components that can hold images, text, and actions like your 'save' button. They are a popular and flexible UI component, especially for responsive design. Use them when you have a list of heterogeneous items that share common behaviors, such as each recipe having an image, title, summary, and a save action. This approach ensures a consistent look and feel, and it's visually pleasing. When designing, mock up the card with the most content and the least content to ensure readability and clarity across different screen sizes. Decide whether your 'save' action will be an icon or a text link, and use real recipe photos to determine the best orientation for the images.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: For our e-commerce website, we're showing product listings. Each product has a main image, name, price, and a 'quick view' button. Users will be browsing on both desktop and mobile. How should I structure this?

Cards are a suitable pattern for your product listings. They are excellent for displaying collections of items where each item might differ in size or aspect ratio, yet they all share associated behaviors like displaying an image, name, price, and offering a 'quick view' action. Cards are widely recognized by users, especially on mobile and web, and offer flexibility across different viewport sizes. To ensure a good design, create mockups for the product with the most and least amount of text or detail to guarantee the information is clear and readable. Also, use actual product images to decide on the best image orientation (portrait or landscape) that works well for your content.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm building a news aggregator app. Each article has a headline, a short snippet, a thumbnail image, and a share button. How can I display these articles effectively?

Cards would be a good choice for your news aggregator app. They are well-suited for presenting lists of items like articles, where each item contains an image, text, and actions such as a share button. This pattern is flexible and works well for responsive designs, accommodating different screen sizes. Since users are likely familiar with cards, they will understand how to interact with your articles. When designing, ensure that your card layout accommodates both the longest and shortest article snippets, and use real images to determine the optimal orientation for thumbnails. Also, consider whether the share button should be an icon or a text link.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a mobile app for a photo-sharing service. Should I make my thumbnails larger or smaller?

Consider the size of the thumbnails in your photo-sharing app. Examples like Google Images and the iPhone home screen show thumbnails that are 'just big enough to be touched easily by human fingertips.' This suggests that for a mobile app where touch interaction is primary, ensuring thumbnails are easily tappable is crucial for usability.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My streaming service's mobile app needs to display a list of movies. What's a good way to present them?

For your mobile streaming app, a 'Thumbnail Grid' is a pattern that can be used to show movies. The HBO Now application, for instance, uses a thumbnail grid featuring a photo and title for each movie or TV show. When designing this grid, pay attention to the relative sizes of the thumbnails, ensuring they are appropriately sized for easy touch interaction on mobile devices.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a mobile app for a small e-commerce business. We want to showcase a few featured products on the home screen, but space is limited. Should I use a carousel?

Yes, a F ilmstrip pattern, which is a variant of a Carousel, is suitable for mobile screens where space is limited. It displays one item at a time, and users can swipe or scroll to view others. This pattern helps users browse through products, similar to how Amazon displays book covers. Consider how much information to provide with each product and how tightly packed the visuals should be to optimize the user experience on a small screen.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We're building a photo gallery app for tablets. Users need to be able to browse through many images. Should we use arrows on our carousel to indicate there's more content?

Yes, providing arrows with your carousel, as seen in examples like Airbnb, can be beneficial. Arrows help users understand that there is more content available beyond the visible window. This is especially useful when browsing through a large number of items, like photos in a gallery, as it signals the discoverability of additional content.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a mobile app with a long, alphabetically sorted list of products. Users often need to find a specific product quickly. What pattern can help?

Consider using the 'Jump to Item' pattern. This pattern is useful when your interface presents a long, sorted list (like a scrolling list or drop-down box) and the user wants to select a particular item quickly and accurately, especially with the keyboard. It allows users to type the name of an item and jump straight to it within the list.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: On our e-commerce website, we have many search results displayed across multiple pages. Users are sometimes confused about where they are in the results and how many pages there are. What should I focus on for the pagination control?

For your pagination control, ensure it provides sufficient information about the user's current location and the total number of screens. Also, pay attention to the size of the click targets. Users need to be able to accurately click on the pagination links, whether using a mouse or a fingertip.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a photo gallery app for mobile. We have a list of user-uploaded photos that are all roughly the same size. We don't have much vertical space, but we want to make browsing these photos engaging. What pattern should I use?

Consider using a Carousel. This pattern arranges visually interesting items, like your photos, into a horizontal strip, allowing users to scroll or swipe through them. It's a good choice when list items have visual representations that uniquely identify them and are similar in size, and when you don't have enough vertical space for a Thumbnail Grid. A Carousel offers an engaging interface for browsing, encouraging users to inspect items and see what's next. However, users can't easily jump to a specific item deep in the list, so they must scroll through everything. This pattern thus encourages browsing and serendipity.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: For our music streaming app's 'New Releases' section on the desktop, we're showing album art. We want users to casually browse these albums, not necessarily search for a specific one. The album art is the main focus, with just the album title and artist name below it. What's the best way to display this?

A Carousel would be suitable here. Use it to arrange album art, which serves as a visual representation, into a horizontal strip. This pattern works well when the picture should take up most of the space devoted to the item, and you only need to show a little metadata like the album title and artist. Users will browse casually, and if they need to find a specific album, they won't mind scrolling past many items. A Carousel offers an engaging interface for browsing and can be a better solution than a Thumbnail Grid if you don't have a lot of vertical space.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm working on a TV interface where we display a list of recommended movies. Each movie has a poster image and a short description. We have a lot of horizontal space but limited vertical space. Users will likely browse these recommendations rather than search for a specific one. How should I lay this out?

A Carousel is a strong candidate for your TV interface. Arrange the movie poster images horizontally, potentially with the album title and artist name below, keeping the metadata in small print to maintain the visual prominence of the posters. Show only a small number of them at once, hiding the rest on either side, and provide large arrows for paging through the Carousel. This pattern is effective when list items have visual representations, are similar in size, and the user will browse casually. It's also compact vertically, making it ideal for limited vertical space.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm building a product listing page for an e-commerce app on iOS. The list of products can be very long. What's the best way to handle this?

You should use pagination to break up the long list of products into manageable pages. This is useful when the list might be very long, as users typically only want to see a few items or find a specific one, not browse the entire list. Pagination prevents overwhelming the user and puts the control of seeing more items in their hands. Ensure the first screen of products is highly relevant, as most users won't go beyond it. Consider disabling 'Previous' when on the first page and 'Next' when on the last, and use numbered links with ellipses for navigation if there are many pages.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We have a search results page on our website, and sometimes the results take too long to load. How can we improve the user experience?

Pagination is a good solution here. Break the search results into pages and load them one at a time. This is crucial if loading the entire list would take too much time, especially over a slow connection or with a slow server. It also helps if rendering the list is time-consuming. The key benefit is that it prevents users from waiting too long and presents them with a digestible chunk of information. Make sure the most relevant results appear on the first page.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: For our video streaming platform, users can browse through a list of recommended videos. Some users want to see more videos than we show by default. Should we use infinite scrolling or pagination?

Given that users might linger on this list and potentially want to see more, pagination is a suitable pattern. It breaks the list into pages, which is easier for users to digest than a single, very long list. It also allows users to choose to load more items. For lists where users might want to see many items, consider letting them set the number of items per screen, as some users find repeatedly scrolling back and forth irritating. Ensure your pagination controls include 'Previous' and 'Next' links, and potentially numbered links with ellipses if there are many pages.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a mobile app with a long list of countries to select from. Users will be on the go. How can I make it easier for them to find their country quickly?

Consider implementing a 'Jump to Item' pattern. When a user types the first letter of their country, the system should automatically scroll to and select the first matching item. This allows users to keep their hands on the keyboard and avoid scrolling or clicking, which is especially helpful when they're on the go. If they type more characters, the selection should refine to the exact match. If there's no match, it's best to stay at the nearest match rather than scrolling back to the top.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We have a desktop application with a very long list of historical stock prices that users need to navigate. What's a good way to help them find specific dates quickly?

For long lists like historical stock prices, the 'Jump to Item' pattern is highly effective. As the user types the first few characters of a year or date, the system can automatically scroll to and select the corresponding item. This leverages the computer's ability to quickly find items in ordered lists, saving the user time and effort compared to manual scrolling. You might also consider an 'Alpha/Numeric Scroller' if users need to jump to very specific points in time, as it provides an interactive map to the list content.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: My e-commerce site has a dropdown for selecting states. It's getting quite long. What's the best way to improve this selection process?

You can improve the state selection dropdown by using the 'Jump to Item' pattern. As the user types the first letter of their state, the list should automatically scroll to and highlight the first matching state. This allows users to quickly find their selection without having to scroll through the entire list, keeping their hands on the keyboard and streamlining the form-filling process.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a mobile app for a recipe book. The list of recipes is very long. How can I help users quickly jump to a specific letter of the alphabet?

You can use the Alphabet Scroller pattern. Place the long list of recipes into a scrolled area and show the letters of the alphabet along the scroll bar. When a user clicks a letter, scroll the list to that point. This pattern is inspired by physical books like dictionaries and is well-known from apps like the iPhone Contacts app. It helps users navigate long lists efficiently.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: In my task management app, I have a list of to-do items. I want to make it super easy for users to add a new task, but I'm running out of space on the screen. What's a good approach?

Consider using the New-Item Row pattern. Place a row at the end of your to-do list specifically for creating new items. This is useful when you have a vertical list of items, need to allow users to add new ones, but have limited UI space for extra buttons. It makes item creation efficient and explicit.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm building a contact list for a new social networking app. Users will be adding many contacts. What's a good way to handle adding new contacts without cluttering the interface?

The New-Item Row pattern would be effective here. Designate the last row of your contact list as the place where users can add a new contact. This pattern is ideal for vertical lists where adding new items is frequent but screen real estate is limited, ensuring a streamlined user experience.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a project management tool where users add tasks to a list. Should I put a 'New Task' button at the top or bottom of the list, or somewhere else?

To make item creation conceptually coherent and elegant, give the user an easy and obvious way to initiate a new object from the first empty table row. A single mouse click in that row might start editing, or the row could contain a 'New Task' button. This uses less screen real estate and reduces navigation, avoiding a jump to another window. The UI should then create the new item in that row, making each column editable to set the task's values.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: In my app, users can create custom tags for their photos. How can I make the process of adding a new tag feel more integrated?

Instead of a separate UI for tag creation, let the user type directly into the end of the tag list. This puts the act of creation where the tag will ultimately live, which is more coherent. The first empty row in the list could be editable, allowing users to start typing their new tag immediately. This reduces the amount of navigation needed and is less work for the user.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: When users add a new entry to a table in my inventory app, what should happen if they click away before finishing?

If a user abandons edits to a new item, it's best to establish a valid item from the beginning. The item should exist until the user explicitly deletes it. Using Good Defaults can help by prefilling valid values, especially if there are multiple fields, so the user doesn't have to edit every column. This approach can resemble an Input Prompt, where a dummy value is presented as a prompt showing the user what to do.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a photo gallery app for mobile. Users are viewing a single photo and I want them to be able to zoom in and out. What's the best gesture for this?

For zooming in and out on a photo in your mobile app, the 'pinch' gesture is the standard interaction. Sliding two fingertips toward each other on the screen will zoom out, and sliding them apart (reverse pinch) will zoom in or magnify the image. This is a common and intuitive gesture for controlling the view on touch screen devices.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: We have a list of emails in our mobile app, and when a user swipes left on an email, we want to reveal options like 'archive' or 'delete'. Is this a good approach?

Yes, using a 'swipe' gesture on a list item to reveal actions like archive or delete is a common and effective pattern in mobile applications. This allows you to present actions that are context-specific to an item without cluttering the main interface. It's a way of navigating or revealing additional options associated with that specific item in the list.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: For our mobile app's main screen, we need a prominent button to guide users to sign up. What kind of button should we use?

On a landing page or startup page for your mobile app, a single, large, eye-catching button is appropriate for a call to action like 'sign up'. These buttons are designed to attract attention and clearly indicate the primary action. They are obvious, easy to use, and serve the purpose of saying 'Click me!' effectively.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: For my mobile app that lets users edit photos, I want to add a 'delete' function. What's the best way to make sure users know they can remove a photo?

To make it clear that users can remove a photo, you should ensure the action is available and well-labeled. Users expect to be able to 'remove or delete something' as a common task. Making this action easy to find and understand will help them get their work done efficiently using interaction methods they already know.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a desktop application for managing project tasks. Users need to be able to add new tasks. How can I best present this action?

For your desktop task management application, ensure the action to 'add or create something' is clearly presented. Users rely on learned behaviors from other applications, so following established conventions for initiating actions will make it easier for them to add new tasks. Make the action available, label it well, and ensure it's easy to find.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: In my web app for scheduling appointments, users need to be able to pause or cancel an ongoing booking process. What should I consider?

When designing the ability for users to 'start, pause, cancel, or complete an action' in your web app, it's crucial to follow established UI standards. Users depend on learned interaction methods from other applications. Therefore, make the pause and cancel actions clearly available, well-labeled, and easy to find to support their task completion.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a photo editing app for desktop. Users are complaining that there are too many buttons visible at once, making the interface cluttered. What can I do?

Consider using Hover Tools. These make action buttons invisible until the user's mouse hovers over an item. This avoids cluttering the page with repeated buttons, especially useful for mouse-driven interfaces. However, be mindful that this pattern doesn't work well for touch screens.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: For my new document management software on desktop, I want to ensure users can quickly perform common actions like saving and opening files. What are some efficient ways to implement this?

Implement Keyboard Shortcuts, such as Ctrl-S for saving. These act as accelerators, allowing experienced users to complete tasks more quickly and reducing mouse movement. They are also critical for accessibility, enabling users who cannot use a mouse to interact with the interface. Major UI platforms have similar standard shortcuts, so align with those conventions.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm building a web application for managing customer data on desktop. Users need to navigate through form fields and submit data efficiently, ideally without using a mouse. What interaction pattern supports this?

Implement Tab Order. This allows users to use the Tab key to move keyboard focus sequentially through all selectable UI components on the screen. Once a component, like a form field or submit button, is selected, it can be modified or 'clicked' using keyboard commands, which is essential for users who rely on keyboard navigation or assistive technologies.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: I'm designing a mobile photo editing app. I want users to be able to delete a photo from a list. What's a good way to do this?

On mobile interfaces, favor acting on objects directly. You can allow users to swipe a list item, like a photo, to delete it. This is a form of direct manipulation, where a gesture directly acts upon the object. Watch out for making this action too easy to trigger accidentally; consider if a confirmation is needed.

source: Designing Interfaces Patterns for Effective Interaction Design - 3rd Edition - Jenifer Tidwell, Charles Brewer, Aynne Valencia - O'Reilly Media (2020).pdf

messages.content: In my new desktop app, I have a button that performs a critical function. How can I make it clear to users that they can click it?

To make an action clear, use affordances. A traditional raised-edge button affords clicking. You can also make an icon or object look different from the rest of the interface, or have it react when the mouse pointer rolls over it.
